frontmatter.title: Probability Applied — A Worked Sensor Noise Model

children.value: From One Random Event to a Gaussian Noise Floor

children.label: From One Random Event to a Gaussian Noise Floor

children.value: Part 6 laid out the generic measurement chain. This page works it through end-to-end for one concrete sensor — the camera — because every link in the chain corresponds to physically distinct hardware, making it the cleanest worked example.
The same template applies to any sensor (vibration, audio, network telemetry); the cross-reference table at the end shows where each one plugs in. This is the applied capstone of the
1. Random Variables and Distributions — Quick Recap

children.label: 1. Random Variables and Distributions — Quick Recap

children.value: is a function that maps an experiment’s outcome to a number. Every sensor reading is a random variable — it takes a different value each time you measure the same underlying state, because the physics of measurement is inherently random.
completely describes a random variable: it assigns a probability to every possible value.
The three quantities that characterise a distribution:

σ2=E[(X−μ)2]\sigma^2 = E[(X-\mu)^2]σ2=E[(X−μ)2]

children.value: In a sensor, multiple noise sources add together (shot noise + read noise + dark current + quantization). Even if each source is non-Gaussian, their sum is approximately Gaussian. The
is why we can model total sensor noise as Gaussian in almost all practical cases — and why the same conclusion holds for a vibration sensor, an audio

children.html: σshot=λ\sigma_{shot} = \sqrt{\lambda}σshot​=λ​

children.value: This cannot be reduced by better hardware — it is a fundamental property of the discrete nature of light. To halve the shot noise you must quadruple the photon count (larger aperture, longer exposure, bigger photosite).

children.html: SNR=λλ=λ\text{SNR} = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \sqrt{\lambda}SNR=λ​λ​=λ​
2≈41%\sqrt{2} \approx 41\%2​≈41%

children.value: . This is the fundamental physical limit for any photon-counting measurement.

children.html: σ=λ\sigma = \sqrt{\lambda}σ=λ​

children.value: rule holds for any Poisson process: bearing-fault counts, packet drops, Geiger counter clicks. In each case, the irreducible relative noise is

children.html: 1/λ1/\sqrt{\lambda}1/λ​

children.value: — the only way to reduce it is to count more events.
A real sensor has multiple noise sources. The total noise variance is their sum (assuming independence):

children.html: σtotal2=σshot2+σread2+σdark2+σquant2\sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{shot}^2 + \sigma_{read}^2 + \sigma_{dark}^2 + \sigma_{quant}^2σtotal2​=σshot2​+σread2​+σdark2​+σquant2​
=λ+σr2+σd2+Δ212= \lambda + \sigma_r^2 + \sigma_d^2 + \frac{\Delta^2}{12}=λ+σr2​+σd2​+12Δ2​
Δ\DeltaΔ

children.value: Which term dominates depends on the signal regime:

children.html: λ≫σr2\lambda \gg \sigma_r^2λ≫σr2​
σ∝λ\sigma \propto \sqrt{\lambda}σ∝λ​
λ≪σr2\lambda \ll \sigma_r^2λ≪σr2​
σ≈σr\sigma \approx \sigma_rσ≈σr​
λ≥C\lambda \geq Cλ≥C
CCC

children.value: In bright conditions, collecting more signal always helps. In dim conditions, electronics dominate and more signal doesn’t help much until you climb out of the electronics floor.
The same regime structure shows up in any measurement chain: an electronics-limited floor at low signal, a physics-limited (often

children.html: λ\sqrt{\lambda}λ​

children.value: ) regime at medium signal, and a saturation regime where the measurement is destroyed.
Poisson noise has variance equal to its mean — which makes it awkward statistically (the noise level depends on the signal level). The
converts Poisson noise to approximately unit-variance Gaussian:

children.html: A(X)=2X+38A(X) = 2\sqrt{X + \frac{3}{8}}A(X)=2X+83​​

children.value: . This lets standard Gaussian-assumption algorithms work correctly on Poisson-distributed data — useful any time you have count data you want to feed into a tool that assumes constant-variance Gaussian noise.
— develops every step on this page from scratch using Monte Carlo simulation. Read those first if you want the build-up; this page is the destination.
